question: When coupling twin trailers, the shut-off valves at the rear of the front trailer should be:
correct_answer: Open
explanation: The shut-off valves at the rear of the front trailer (and at the rear of the dolly) should be open to allow air to reach the brakes on the second trailer.

question: When coupling doubles or triples, the trailer height should be:
options: Higher than the center of the fifth wheel
Slightly lower than the center of the fifth wheel
correct_answer: Slightly lower than the center of the fifth wheel
explanation: The trailer should be slightly lower than the center of the fifth wheel so that when you back under it, the trailer will be lifted slightly as the fifth wheel locks into place.

question: The converter dolly air tank drain valve should be:
options: At the front of the dolly
At the rear of the dolly
On the side of the dolly
There is no drain valve on a dolly
correct_answer: At the rear of the dolly
explanation: The converter dolly air tank drain valve is located at the rear of the dolly and should be drained regularly to remove moisture and contaminants.

question: When backing a trailer, you should:
options: Turn the steering wheel in the direction you want the trailer to go
Turn the steering wheel in the opposite direction you want the trailer to go
correct_answer: Turn the steering wheel in the opposite direction you want the trailer to go
explanation: When backing a trailer, turn the steering wheel in the opposite direction you want the trailer to go. This is opposite of backing a car.

question: The air leakage rate for a combination vehicle with the engine off and brakes applied should not exceed:
correct_answer: 4 psi in one minute
explanation: For a combination vehicle (tractor-trailer), the air loss rate with the engine off and brakes applied should not exceed 4 psi in one minute.

question: Before coupling, the fifth wheel should be:
options: Tilted down toward the rear of the tractor
Tilted up toward the rear of the tractor
correct_answer: Tilted down toward the rear of the tractor
explanation: The fifth wheel should be tilted down toward the rear of the tractor so that when you back under the trailer, the kingpin will slide smoothly into the fifth wheel.

question: When coupling doubles, after connecting the converter dolly to the front trailer, the dolly support should be:
correct_answer: Raised to the up position
explanation: After connecting the converter dolly to the front trailer, raise the dolly support to the up position to allow the dolly to move freely.

question: The pintle hook on the rear of the first trailer should be:
correct_answer: Latched and locked
explanation: The pintle hook (or coupling device) on the rear of the first trailer must be properly latched and locked to securely connect to the converter dolly.

question: Doubles and triples are more likely to turn over than other vehicles because:
options: The rear trailer tends to swing out in a turn (crack the whip effect)
correct_answer: The rear trailer tends to swing out in a turn (crack the whip effect)
explanation: In doubles and triples, the rear trailer can swing out sharply in turns, especially if you turn too quickly. This 'crack the whip' effect can cause rollovers.

question: When inspecting doubles or triples, make sure the shut-off valves:
options: At the rear of the last trailer are open, and all others are closed
At the rear of the last trailer are closed, and all others are open
correct_answer: At the rear of the last trailer are closed, and all others are open
explanation: All shut-off valves should be open except the ones at the rear of the last trailer, which should be closed to maintain air pressure in the system.

question: The trailer air supply control should be used to:
options: Supply air to the trailer brakes
Release the trailer brakes for normal driving
Apply the trailer brakes in an emergency
Both supply air and release the trailer brakes
correct_answer: Both supply air and release the trailer brakes
explanation: The trailer air supply control (also called the tractor protection valve control) supplies air to the trailer brakes and releases the trailer emergency brakes for normal driving.

question: When coupling, after backing under the trailer, you should:
options: Check that the kingpin is locked into the fifth wheel
correct_answer: Check that the kingpin is locked into the fifth wheel
explanation: After backing under the trailer, check that the kingpin is properly locked into the fifth wheel before proceeding. This ensures a secure connection.

question: The service line (also called the control line or signal line) carries:
options: Air to control the trailer brakes
correct_answer: Air to control the trailer brakes
explanation: The service line (blue) carries air controlled by the brake pedal to apply the trailer brakes during normal driving.

question: The emergency line (also called the supply line) is:
correct_answer: Red
explanation: The emergency line (supply line) is red. It supplies air to the trailer air tanks and controls the emergency brakes on the trailer.

question: Before uncoupling, you should:
correct_answer: Lower the landing gear
explanation: Before uncoupling, lower the landing gear to support the trailer. Make sure it is fully extended and the crank handle is secured.

question: When pulling doubles or triples, you should:
options: Allow more space for stopping and turning
Use cruise control at all times
correct_answer: Allow more space for stopping and turning
explanation: Doubles and triples require more space for stopping and turning. Allow extra following distance and take turns more slowly to prevent rollovers.

question: A converter dolly is used to:
options: Connect the tractor to the first trailer
Support the front of the second or third trailer
correct_answer: Support the front of the second or third trailer
explanation: A converter dolly (or dolly) is a coupling device with one or two axles and a fifth wheel used to support the front of a second or third trailer.

question: When coupling twin trailers, you should secure the second trailer by connecting the:
options: Converter dolly to the rear of the first trailer
correct_answer: Converter dolly to the rear of the first trailer
explanation: Secure the second trailer by properly connecting the converter dolly to the rear of the first trailer using the pintle hook or coupling device.

question: The maximum length for a double trailer combination on most highways is:
correct_answer: Varies by state
explanation: Maximum length limits for double and triple trailers vary by state. Check state regulations before operating in a new state.

question: When coupling, after connecting the air lines, you should:
options: Charge the trailer air system and check for leaks
correct_answer: Charge the trailer air system and check for leaks
explanation: After connecting the air lines, charge the trailer air system by pushing in the trailer air supply knob. Then check for air leaks before driving.

question: The glad hands are used to:
options: Connect the air lines between the tractor and trailer
correct_answer: Connect the air lines between the tractor and trailer
explanation: Glad hands are coupling devices used to connect the service and emergency air lines between the tractor and trailer or between trailers.

question: When driving doubles or triples, you should check the coupling devices:
options: Before the trip and during the trip
Only when you feel a problem
correct_answer: Before the trip and during the trip
explanation: Check all coupling devices before the trip and regularly during the trip to ensure they remain secure and properly connected.

question: The safety chains or cables should be:
options: Crossed under the tongue of the dolly
correct_answer: Crossed under the tongue of the dolly
explanation: Safety chains or cables should be crossed under the tongue of the dolly to provide support if the coupling fails.

question: When uncoupling twin trailers, you should:
options: Disconnect both at the same time
correct_answer: Disconnect the rear trailer first
explanation: When uncoupling twin trailers, disconnect the rear trailer first, then the converter dolly, and finally the front trailer.

question: Rearward amplification (crack the whip effect) is:
options: More pronounced in longer combination vehicles
Only a problem in single trailers
Not a concern for experienced drivers
correct_answer: More pronounced in longer combination vehicles
explanation: Rearward amplification (crack the whip effect) is more pronounced in longer combination vehicles. The rear trailer can swing out much more than the tractor during turns or lane changes.
